summary: VDP-163 is a revised funding proposal for developing antisense oligonucleotide (AON) therapeutics targeting FOXO3 isoforms to treat degenerative disc disease (DDD). The project aims to design and validate AONs that shift the balance from FOXO3-short (enriched in aging/degenerating tissues) to FOXO3-long (health-promoting) isoforms. Phase 1 includes AON design, 2D cell culture validation, and ex vivo organ-on-chip testing in human disc cells. Budget: £219,846 ($297,805) over ~18 months with a go/no-go decision point after initial validation.

content_blocks.title: Project Overview and Revision Notes
content_blocks.content: One-liner: RNA therapeutic targeting FOXO3 isoforms for degenerative disc disease (DDD) to extend healthspan. Revision from VDP-146 (approved on Discourse but failed quorum). Key changes: (1) prioritizes DDD as go-to-market indication, (2) includes intermediate go/no-go milestone capping initial risk at half budget, (3) VitaDAO owns developed IP rather than licensing, (4) plans IPT raise with $50k anchor from VitaDAO, (5) SpineDAO expressed interest to co-fund, (6) budget increased $50k to advance asset further, (7) removed C. elegans testing. PI: Lorna Harries, PhD (Exeter University). Posted August 1, 2025 for community comment before Snapshot vote.

content_blocks.title: Problem Statement: FOXO3 and DDD
content_blocks.content: FOXO3 is a validated longevity gene (one haplotype associated with ~36% increased chance of exceptional longevity across >11 populations) but has never been successfully drugged. It orchestrates autophagy, mitochondrial quality control, DNA repair, metabolic flexibility, and inflammatory restraint. Previous agonist approaches were non-specific and affected multiple pathways (AKT, PI3K, AMPK). Lower back pain from DDD is the leading cause of physical disability; 12-16% of adults visit GP annually for back pain (UK: 3.5M incident, 17.3M prevalent cases; 116M production days lost/year). Current standard of care is steroids (adverse effects, poor long-term outcomes) or surgery (invasive, costly, infection risk, mortality in elderly, not durable—e.g., arthrodesis accelerates adjacent disc degeneration). No disease-modifying options exist.

content_blocks.title: Solution: FOXO3 Isoform Modulation via Oligonucleotides
content_blocks.content: Harries' team discovered that longevity-associated FOXO3 genetic variation is mediated by alternatively spliced mRNA isoforms. FOXO3 expresses 3 linear mRNAs + 1 circular RNA. FOXO3-long encodes complete fork-head DNA-binding domain (ubiquitous); FOXO3-short lacks key regulatory motifs and is enriched in aging muscle and degenerating tissues. Aging/metabolic stress shift balance toward short form, diminishing stress responses and tissue integrity. Restoring youthful long-to-short ratio is a mechanistically grounded gerotherapeutic entry point. Oligonucleotide modality: Use antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) for steric hindrance of isoform-specific elements or small activating RNAs (saRNAs). ASOs proven safe/effective in clinic (SMA, DMD). Based on PI's 30 years RNA biology experience and her role as Founder/CSO of SENISCA Ltd (oligonucleotide company). Oligos are fastest-growing drug class (4 new approvals in 2023), highly specific (20-25 nucleotide target region), can be delivered naked in saline locally, allow drugging 'undruggable' targets like transcription factors. FOXO3 expression strongly correlates with DDD in mouse and human; associated with extracellular matrix degradation and nucleus pulposus cell death. FOXO3 overexpression ameliorates disc degeneration by decreasing NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis. Long and short isoforms play unique roles in bone remodeling; short isoforms suppress bone remodeling. Therapeutics delivered by direct intradiscal injection (standard route). Oligos have long duration (up to 6 months). Delivered naked (no nanoparticles); PI has track record delivering oligos to human primary cells by gymnosis.

content_blocks.title: Opportunity: DDD as Lead Indication
content_blocks.content: DDD chosen as exemplar disease for gerodrug demonstration. Rationale: (1) published research connects FOXO3 with DDD, (2) local delivery via familiar route, (3) less heterogeneous presentation than cancer/diabetes/CVD, (4) large/growing market ($35B in 2025 → $45B by 2029, 7.5% CAGR), (5) less crowded competitive space than cancer/CVD/diabetes. Competitive landscape: Other assets are non-disease-modifying (hydrogels), non-specific (TGFβ targeting with tolerability concerns), or biologicals (stem cells with limited availability, high cost, rejection risk). Targeted AON has precise MoA, limited off-target effects, improved safety with local administration (limited systemic exposure). Future label expansion potential to other age-related diseases where FOXO3 dysregulation plays role.

content_blocks.title: FOXO3 Target Disease Opportunities
content_blocks.content: Comparison table of disease areas: DDD/IDD (advantages: local intradiscal injection avoids systemic PK, moderately crowded pipeline ~15 assets vs >100 in CVD, validated disc height & pain endpoints; challenges: chronic low-back-pain space still competitive with cell/hydrogel/peptide trials; market: $35B global spine-pain therapeutics & surgery). CVD ($160B market, highly crowded >100 late-phase drugs, requires systemic delivery). Neurodegenerative disorders ($20B market, orphan incentives for ALS/HD, blood-brain-barrier delivery challenge, high trial failure rate, heterogeneous patients). Cancer ($200B market, extremely crowded, heterogeneous patients, complex safety). Autoimmune/immune dysfunction (US $170B market, chronic high-value biologic segment, Humira-class biologics dominate, heterogeneous patients, long systemic exposure, infection risk). Sarcopenia/frailty ($4B projected, first-in-class potential, regulatory endpoints debated SPPB/gait speed, requires systemic delivery). Diabetes & complications ($40B market, very crowded GLP-1/SGLT2/Tirzepatide boom, requires chronic systemic dosing, lifestyle/obesity confounders). All include FOXO3 references numbered 12-17 in bibliography.

content_blocks.title: Relevance to Longevity and IP Roadmap
content_blocks.content: By rebalancing FOXO3 long/short isoforms, project targets central node orchestrating autophagy, mitochondrial maintenance, DNA repair, anti-inflammatory signaling—processes slowing functional decline across multiple tissues. DDD focus offers clear clinical path, but same antisense strategy can later be retargeted systemically or to other organs for sarcopenia, vascular aging, immune dysfunction, metabolic disease. Modulates evolutionarily conserved hub rather than single downstream pathology, holding potential to extend healthspan (period free from chronic disability) and lifespan. IP Roadmap: Phase 1 output = prototype oligonucleotides + demonstration of efficacy in 2D and 3D tissue culture systems attenuating senescence/disease markers. Sufficient data for initial patent filing. Following filing, claims bolstered by in vivo data within 12 months (contingent on further funding).

content_blocks.title: Experimental Plan Phase 1: Milestones 1a and 1b
content_blocks.content: Hypothesis: Targeted moderation of FOXO3 isoform expression attenuates cellular aging and tissue health parameters in vitro, ex vivo, and in vivo. MILESTONE 1a - AON Design and Validation in Monolayer Culture: Objective: Design/validate ASOs that effectively/specifically modulate FOXO3 isoforms in human primary fibroblasts (NHDF), Human Nucleus Pulposus Cells (HNPC), Human Annulus Fibrosus Cells (HAFC). Workplan: Design ASOs using steric hindrance translation blockers against isoform-specific translation start sites of FOXO3-short or -long, or block negative regulatory elements to manipulate expression. Standard technologies routinely used in Harries lab and SENISCA (several oligos with similar MoA already developed). Measure isoform switching efficiency by Western blot (band size/intensity using anti-FOXO3 antibody binding epitope in both isoforms) and RT-qPCR. Generate cells expressing primarily FOXO3-long, primarily FOXO3-short, or both. Outcome: Identify ≥2 lead AON candidates with potent/specific FOXO3 isoform modulation and reduced senescence markers. Provides foundational evidence of correct molecular outcome. MILESTONE 1b - Evaluate AON Efficacy in 2D Culture: Objective: Determine if FOXO3 isoform changes translate to functional rescue of senescent phenotype in DDD-relevant cells. Test if AONs (1) alter FOXO3 activity and subcellular localization, (2) rescue DDD phenotypes including autophagy, mitochondrial function, senescent cell accumulation. Workplan: Assess subcellular localization of FOXO3-long and -short proteins in NHDF, HNPC, HAFC by immunofluorescence. Induce degenerative DDD-like state using TNFα and IL-1β under serum-limiting conditions. Measure FOXO3 isoform ability to transactivate commercially available luciferase reporter with FOXO binding sites. Assess mitochondrial function via live cell respiration/glycolysis on Seahorse XF24 platform. Measure senescence rate: SA-beta-Gal activity, EdU incorporation (proliferation), gamma-H2AX (DNA damage foci), autophagy using Operetta high-content imager with unique machine-learning algorithm for senescent cell classification/quantification. Assess SASP, molecular senescence markers (CDKN2A/p16, CDKN1A/p21, TP53/p53, LAMINB1), disease-associated features (MMP9, MMP13, MMP3, ACAN) by qPCR and ELISA. Outcome: Validation of AON impact on FOXO3 activity/localization, mitochondrial function, senescence kinetics, autophagy, cell survival in human primary cell types relevant to DDD in 2D.

content_blocks.title: Go/No-Go Decision Point and Milestone 2
content_blocks.content: → Go/No-Go Point After Milestones 1a and 1b: Decision based on successful achievement: specifically, identification of AONs that robustly shift FOXO3 isoform ratios and produce statistically significant improvement in ≥2 key cellular health markers (e.g., reduced senescence, increased matrix production). MILESTONE 2 - Evaluate AON Efficacy in Human Ex Vivo Model: Objective: Evaluate AONs targeting FOXO3 isoforms in physiologically relevant ex vivo DDD model. Workplan: Assess AON impact in ex vivo organotypic organ-on-a-chip cultures (and if available, primary human IVD explants). Platform incorporates human primary nucleus pulposus (NP) and annulus fibrosus (AF) cells induced to DDD-like phenotype using TNF-α and IL-1β. Organ-on-chip enables precise spatial arrangement replicating native disc architecture (NP cells in center, AF cells at periphery), co-culture with stromal fibroblasts. Evaluate paracrine signaling effects of aged/damaged cells on unconnected populations. Assess disease-relevant outcomes: expression of collagens, fibromodulin, IL-6, IL-8, aggrecan (ACAN), MMP13, MMP9, MMP3, plus morphological analysis. Outcome: Validation data demonstrating AON impact on molecular/structural features of disc degeneration in human-relevant ex vivo system, supporting further therapeutic development.

content_blocks.title: Diagrammatic Workplan Figure 3
content_blocks.content: Figure shows workflow: (1) Targeting sequences identified → Design AON (MILESTONE 1a) → branches to (2a) Evaluate in vitro (MILESTONE 1b) with target engagement graph and cell physiology icon, and (2b) Evaluate ex vivo (MILESTONE 2) with organ-on-chip illustration.

content_blocks.title: Go/No-Go Milestones for Phase 2 and Phase 2 Plan
content_blocks.content: Go/No Go Milestones for Phase 2: (1) Identification of prototype oligo producing desired effect on FOXO3 isoform usage/activity in 5nM to 400nM range. (2) Identification of prototype oligo with beneficial effects on cellular aging and disease markers in 2D monocultures and 3D human primary cell organoid models of DDD. Phase 2: Translation Round (subsequent fundraise): First steps = optimize oligo sequence and chemistries. Routinely done by PI as CSO of SENISCA. Deduce optimal sequence by 'walking' oligo base-by-base across target. Test lead sequence as different nucleoside/backbone moieties at different dosages (typically 25nM-400nM) in vitro for target engagement. In Vivo PK/PD and Toxicology Studies (CRO): Use appropriate rodent DDD model (lumbar spine instability, chemonucleosis, compression models available). Established CRO network through SENISCA. Outcome measures: macroscopic pain/behavior tests, imaging, molecular features of inflammation/senescence/matrix remodeling. Standard toxicology assessment. CMC and toxicology packages (traditionally 2 mammalian species) undertaken later for AON drugs than small molecules, as test item must match IND package exactly. PK/PD work may require mouse-specific oligo if sequence not conserved, but conventional workflow for non-conserved oligos. Toxicology and CMC begin once candidate molecule identified.

content_blocks.title: Phase 2 Further Funding Diagram
content_blocks.content: Diagram shows: Top row: PK/PD and tox in NHP ← in vivo bioD and toxicology → (oligo sequences with different chemistries) ← Evaluate in vitro → Optimise sequence and chemistry. Bottom row: PK/PD in mouse → Ex vivo human tissue explants. Label: PHASE 2 - FURTHER FUNDING.

content_blocks.title: Path to Clinical Figure 4
content_blocks.content: Timeline showing development phases: 2025: Oligo design and validation, In vitro dataset. 2026: Ex vivo dataset, In vivo dataset. 2027: Rodent PK/PD study. 2028: Oligo sequence and chemistry optimisation, Second animal PK/PD study (NHP). 2029: IND and CMC, Human phase 1. Arrow labeled 'This funding' spans 2025-2026. Arrow labeled 'Continuation funding' (dotted) spans 2026-2029+. Initial filing marked between 2026-2027. IND application marked at ~2029.

content_blocks.title: Figure 4 Caption and Budget Introduction
content_blocks.content: Caption: Path to clinical for new oligonucleotide geroprotective drug. Initial phases (funded by initial investment) cover production of prototype oligonucleotides (patent-protectable) and evaluation in vitro, ex vivo, in vivo. Next steps: optimize sequence/chemistry, determine biodistribution/gross toxicology in higher animal model (usually rodents) to confirm naked oligo delivery in saline to target cells/organs. Then pursue traditional preclinical package: CMC, safety toxicology, PK/PD. Nature/animal models depend on earlier work outcomes; some oligos conserved (assayable in rodents), others primate-specific (require non-human primates). Milestones and estimated timing to IND approval given.

content_blocks.title: Budget Phase 1 Milestones 1a and 1b
content_blocks.content: Staffing: PI Lorna Harries project management 2hr/week £18,371 ($24,885); PDRA to carry out work £70,124 ($94,989). Reagents Milestone 1a: AON oligos 8 sequences £200 each = £1,600 ($2,168); General cellular/molecular consumables £2,500 ($3,387); Oligofectamine £1,600 ($2,168); Primary cells AF and NP £2,200 ($2,981); Specialist tissue culture reagents £3,000 ($4,064); qPCR £1,000 ($1,355); Laptop for PDRA £2,000 ($2,710). Reagents Milestone 1b: Agilent Seahorse XF Cell Mito Stress Test Kit 6×96-well plates £700 ($949); Antibodies £1,250 ($1,694); EdU kits £500 ($678); H2Ax kits £600 ($813); Immunofluorescence consumables £750 ($1,016); Luciferase reporter kits (2) £1,200 ($1,626); ELISA £1,500 ($2,032); SAB kits £500 ($678); TUNEL £900 ($1,219); Western blotting £500 ($678); qPCR £1,000 ($1,355); Specialist tissue culture reagents £3,000 ($4,064). SUBTOTAL: £114,795 ($155,509). → Go/No-Go Point at End of Year 1.

content_blocks.title: Budget Milestone 2 Ex Vivo and Grand Total
content_blocks.content: Milestone 2 - Evaluation of AONs Ex Vivo: Staffing: PI Lorna Harries 2hr/week £18,371 ($24,885); PDRA £70,124 ($94,989). Reagents: OOC system £7,806 ($10,570); Antibodies £1,250 ($1,693); Immunofluorescence consumables £750 ($1,016); ELISA £1,500 ($2,032); SAB kits £500 ($678); LDH £250 ($339); Western blotting £500 ($678); Specialist tissue culture reagents £4,000 ($5,416). SUBTOTAL: £105,051 ($142,296). GRAND TOTAL: £219,846 or $297,805.

content_blocks.title: Financing and VitaDAO Terms
content_blocks.content: Project financed through Intellectual Property Tokens (IPTs) on Bio Protocol platform, anchored by $50,000 commitment from VitaDAO on same terms as all contributors, including aligned DAOs like SpineDAO. Funding terms: agreement for contract research services with Exeter University, granting Vitality Now (Swiss association representing VitaDAO) ownership of intellectual property developed by project.

content_blocks.title: Team: Professor Lorna Harries
content_blocks.content: PhD from University College London 1994. Established RNA-mediated disease mechanisms group at Exeter 2006. Personal Chair in Molecular Genetics at University of Exeter Medical School. Co-founder, co-director, Chief Scientific Officer at SENISCA Ltd (spin-out from Harries lab research). Awards: Diabetes UK RD Lawrence rising star 2011, Queen's Anniversary Prize team member 2006 and direct recipient 2019, Proteomass Lifetime Career Award 2021 for senescence/RNA processing work. Lab interests: -omics approaches to ageing/age-related disease in humans. >160 peer-reviewed publications, >20,000 citations, H-index 56. Proven track record translating basic research; inventor on 2 patents to date, 7 more in draft.

content_blocks.title: Research Team
content_blocks.content: Scientist appointed (PDRA) exclusively focused on this project. Benefits from wider Harries academic team: 7 individuals with multidisciplinary skills (primary cell biology, multi-omics, organ-on-chip systems, bioinformatics, molecular genetics). Complemented by co-location with Harries R&D team at SENISCA (Lorna is Founder/CSO). SENISCA headcount 16 people, specializes in oligonucleotide drug development.

content_blocks.title: References (selected key papers)
content_blocks.content: Key references include: (2) Willcox et al PNAS 2008 - FOXO3A genotype strongly associated with human longevity. (6) Alvarez-Garcia et al Aging Cell 2018 - FOXO required for intervertebral disk homeostasis during aging, deficiency promotes disk degeneration. (11) Frankum et al Geroscience 2021 - Extreme longevity variants at FOXO3 locus may moderate FOXO3 isoform levels. (18) Alvarez-Garcia et al J Orthop Res 2017 - Age-related reduction in FOXO expression correlates with intervertebral disc degeneration. (19) Tan et al Pharmaceuticals 2023 - FOXO3-activated circFGFBP1 inhibits ECM degradation and nucleus pulposus cell death via miR-9-5p/BMP2 axis in IVD degeneration. (20) Min et al Int Immunopharmacol 2025 - FOXO3a overexpression ameliorates IVD degeneration by decreasing NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis. (22) Stein et al Nucleic Acids Res 2010 - Efficient gene silencing by locked nucleic acid ASOs, unassisted by transfection reagents. (24) Bennett Annu Rev Med 2019 - Therapeutic ASOs coming of age. (27) Ponnappan et al Arthritis Res Ther 2011 - Organ culture system to model early degenerative changes of IVD.

content_blocks.title: Community Comment: bowtiedshrike
content_blocks.content: Comment August 6, 2025: Good job improving proposal since last VDP. Organ-on-a-chip makes it stronger, especially in current US regulatory environment. May not need NHP as model if human biomarkers can be used instead.

content_blocks.title: Community Comment: Suhail
content_blocks.content: Comment August 9, 2025: Clinically, most current disc degeneration treatments only mask symptoms; this could tackle root cause. Likes phased funding and VitaDAO owning IP—good mix of ambition and risk control. Questions: (1) How will safety be tracked long-term given FOXO3 role in many body systems? (2) Will there be early biomarkers to prove it's working before big trials? Overall looks like solid bet for impact and learning.

content_blocks.title: PI Response to Comments (Lorna Harries)
content_blocks.content: Response August 19, 2025 to bowtiedshrike: Agrees human-relevant model systems have superior predictive power vs standard animal studies. FDA Modernisation Act 3.0 phases out animal work for IND submissions over coming years. Some animal work necessary for basic toxicology/safety currently, but much can be done computationally, in human tissues ex vivo, organ-on-chip. Animal models often not informative for PD outcomes. Use of relevant human ex vivo tissues/biomarkers often superior approach (adheres to this in role as AFRUK Animal Free Centre of Excellence lead). Will monitor developments and work accordingly.

content_blocks.title: PI Response on Safety and Biomarkers (Lorna Harries)
content_blocks.content: Response August 19, 2025 to Suhail on safety: Safety is priority. Important distinction: not targeting FOXO3 in entirety, but targeted, easily reversible, subtle manipulation of isoform balance, *only* at site of action. Beauty of precisely targeted oligonucleotide drug delivered locally. Not inducing global supraphysiological FOXO3 expression (as some small molecules may), but *restoring* disordered isoform balance only in affected tissue. Oligo allows very precise targeting and, unlike gene therapy, only for period of time—easy to discontinue if adverse impact. Local delivery provides protection against wider impacts. Local oligo administration yields little systemic exposure; effects confined to injection site/environs, safeguarding against body-wide adverse effects. Long-term safety assessed by tracking established markers: cardiac, hepatic, renal, respiratory health (readily measured in clinic). Given FOXO3 metabolism role, also monitor metabolic endpoints (glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, glycation markers). Modality-related toxicity: Most oligo toxicity relates to nanoparticle carriers (not needed here), rare but related to inflammation, liver tox, complement activation. Defined sequence motifs predict this; screened during oligo optimization along with off-target prediction. Before clinic (some way off), asset screened in vivo and/or appropriate ex vivo models (DILI chips etc). Many molecules examined for aging effects act on/intersect FOXO upstream regulators (Trametinib/MEK, AKT inhibitors, SIRT1 agonists, mTOR inhibitors); long-term safety profiles already well understood. Biomarkers: No clinically recognized circulating biomarkers for DDD currently, but emerging area. Promising candidates gaining traction: GDF-15, IL-6, miR-155, miR-26a, ACAN, MMP2, MMP3, MMP7 (PMIDs 38156062, 34954738, 31770330). Markers shown to have predictive/prognostic power for disease progression rate, readily measured in blood. Used in conjunction with established clinical endpoints: spine MRI, CT, pain/mobility assessment.

content_blocks.title: Related Topics Section
content_blocks.content: Links to related VitaDAO proposals: VDP-146 (original FOXO3 oligos proposal, 19 replies, June 2024), VDP-48 (Ikaria Therapeutics assessment, 7 replies, June 2022), VDP-29 (Exosomes for systemic inflammation, 17 replies, July 2022), VDP-18 (Jonathan An periodontal disease using geroscience, 14 replies, March 2022), VDP-16 (Korolchuk Lab autophagy activators, 9 replies, January 2022).

key_findings: FOXO3 longevity gene haplotype associated with ~36% increased chance of exceptional longevity across >11 populations, but never successfully drugged due to lack of specificity in prior small molecule approaches

PI Harries discovered longevity association mediated by alternatively spliced FOXO3 isoforms: FOXO3-long (health-promoting, full DNA-binding domain) vs FOXO3-short (lacks regulatory motifs, enriched in aging/degenerating tissues)

FOXO3 expression strongly correlates with degenerative disc disease (DDD) in mouse and human; FOXO3 overexpression ameliorates disc degeneration by decreasing NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis in published studies

DDD market large and growing ($35B 2025 → $45B 2029, 7.5% CAGR) with less crowded competitive space than cancer/CVD/diabetes; no disease-modifying treatments exist (only symptom management via steroids or surgery)

Phase 1 experimental plan: Design ASOs using steric hindrance against isoform-specific translation start sites or negative regulatory elements; validate in human primary fibroblasts, nucleus pulposus cells, annulus fibrosus cells in 2D culture; assess target engagement (Western blot, RT-qPCR), functional rescue (mitochondrial respiration via Seahorse, senescence markers SA-beta-Gal/EdU/gamma-H2AX, autophagy via Operetta high-content imaging with ML algorithm, disease markers ACAN/MMP3/MMP9/MMP13); test in ex vivo organ-on-chip with native disc architecture
Go/no-go decision after Milestones 1a+1b based on AONs that robustly shift isoform ratios + statistically significant improvement in ≥2 cellular health markers

Local intradiscal delivery route (standard, familiar to patients/clinicians) with naked oligo (no nanoparticles needed based on PI's SENISCA experience); oligos have long duration of action (up to 6 months); local delivery limits systemic exposure and off-target effects
IP strategy: Phase 1 yields prototype oligos + in vitro/ex vivo efficacy data sufficient for initial patent filing; claims bolstered by in vivo data within 12 months (Phase 2, separate fundraise)

Budget £219,846 ($297,805) over ~18 months with intermediate go/no-go checkpoint; VitaDAO owns developed IP (not licensing); IPT raise on Bio Protocol with $50k VitaDAO anchor + SpineDAO co-funding interest

PI credentials: Lorna Harries, PhD (UCL 1994), Chair in Molecular Genetics at Exeter, Founder/CSO of SENISCA Ltd (oligo drug company), >160 publications, >20,000 citations, H-index 56, 2 granted patents + 7 in draft, 30 years RNA biology experience, co-located academic team (7 people) + SENISCA R&D team (16 people)
